US Bankruptcy Filings¶

Trends, Drivers & Geographic Concentration¶

A Data-Driven Analysis for Risk Professionals¶


Data through: Q1 2026  ·  Source: US Courts + FRED  ·  Coverage: 38 quarters, 2016 Q3 – 2026 Q1

Executive Summary — Q1 2026¶
591,850 annual filings (12-month rolling) +11.9% YoY — 4th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth
9,941 Chapter 11 filings All-time high in the dataset; +12.4% YoY
94.4% of Ch.11 are business filings Structural shift driven by the Small Business Reorganization Act (2020)
49 of 51 states posted YoY increases Breadth signals systemic pressure, not regional shocks

Filings remain ~25% below pre-pandemic levels (~800,000 in 2016–2019). This is normalization — not a crisis wave. But momentum is re-accelerating.

The Recovery Trajectory¶

12-Month Rolling National Filings · 2016–2026

In [2]:
show('01_national_total.png')

Three Phases of the Cycle¶

Phase Period Filings Driver
Pre-pandemic baseline 2016–2019 ~800,000 Normal credit cycle
Stimulus suppression 2020–2022 380,634 (trough) PPP, stimulus checks, moratoriums
Normalization 2023–present 591,850 Debt service pressure resuming

The March 2022 trough of 395,373 was the lowest filing volume in modern records — an economy where fiscal support temporarily overwhelmed the underlying distress signal.

Sustained Double-Digit Growth¶

Year-over-Year Change in 12-Month Rolling Filings

In [3]:
show('01_yoy_growth.png')

Quarterly Momentum (3-Month Snapshots)¶

Same-Quarter Prior-Year (SQPY) growth removes seasonal distortion

Quarter 3-Mo Filings QoQ SQPY Ch.11 Ch.11 SQPY
Q4 2024 127,921 −4.6% +11.5% 2,085 −5.7%
Q1 2025 134,063 +4.8% +9.6% 2,039 −2.9%
Q2 2025 146,162 +9.0% +10.1% 2,138 −17.0%
Q3 2025 148,821 +1.8% +11.0% 2,659 +26.3%
Q4 2025 144,620 −2.8% +13.1% 2,355 +12.9%
Q1 2026 152,262 +5.3% +13.6% 2,784 +36.5%

After moderating through mid-2025, SQPY is re-accelerating to +13.6%. Ch.11 SQPY at +36.5% is the strongest reading in 12 months.

Chapter Composition — Q1 2026¶

591,850 annual filings

In [4]:
show('01_chapter_mix.png')

Chapter Breakdown¶

Chapter Filings Share Nature
Ch. 7 369,702 62.5% Liquidation — immediate discharge
Ch. 13 211,700 35.8% Individual reorganization (3–5 yr plan)
Ch. 11 9,941 1.7% Business restructuring
Other ~507 0.1% Agricultural, cross-border

The Ch.7 / Ch.13 ratio varies sharply by geography — a key portfolio risk signal (see Geographic section).

Chapter 11: The Corporate Stress Indicator¶

Annual Ch.11 Filings — All-Time High in Dataset

In [5]:
show('05_ch11_national_trend.png')

Ch.11: Business vs. Non-Business¶

In [6]:
show('05_ch11_biz_split.png')

The SBRA Structural Shift¶

Filings Share
Business Ch.11 9,385 94.4%
Non-business Ch.11 556 5.6%

The Small Business Reorganization Act (SBRA, Feb 2020) created Subchapter V — a streamlined, lower-cost reorganization track for businesses with debts under $7.5M.

Subchapter V dramatically reduced the cost and complexity barrier to Ch.11 for small businesses. The pre-2020 Ch.11 baseline is obsolete as a benchmark.

What Actually Predicts Bankruptcy Filings¶

Correlation with 13 FRED Macroeconomic Series

In [7]:
show('02_correlation_heatmap.png', figsize=(10, 6))

The Predictors That Matter¶

Series Correlation
Mortgage Delinquency Rate +0.77 ✅ Strongest predictor
Household Debt Service Ratio +0.71 ✅
Moody's Baa Credit Spread +0.40 Moderate
Unemployment Rate +0.08 ⚠️ Near zero
Federal Funds Rate −0.34 ⚠️ Weak

Bankruptcy is a debt-stock event, not a job-loss event. Accumulated obligations become unserviceable independent of employment status. Models that lead with unemployment will systematically mis-time the cycle.

The 2020–2022 Stimulus Distortion¶

In [8]:
show('02_macro_overlay.png')

⚠️ Model calibration warning: PPP loans, stimulus checks, eviction moratoriums, and student loan forbearance collectively suppressed filings to 50-year lows. Exclude 2020–2022 from long-run parameter estimation.

Geographic Distribution¶

Top States by Annual Filing Volume · Q1 2026

In [9]:
show('06_state_totals.png')

Top 10 states = ~56% of national filings. Florida +22.3% and Texas +22.2% are the fastest-growing large states.

The North–South Chapter Split¶

Chapter Mix by State

In [10]:
show('06_state_chapter_mix.png')

Southern States: A Different Bankruptcy Culture¶

Region Ch.13 Rate
Alabama, Tennessee, Georgia 50–70% Ch.13 dominant
West Coast, New England 15–30% Ch.7 dominant
National average ~36%

Southern bankruptcy bar culture historically favors Ch.13 reorganization over Ch.7 liquidation — reflecting trustee practices, judicial culture, and home-ownership defense (Ch.13 allows curing mortgage arrears; Ch.7 does not).

Portfolio implication: Southern borrowers who file extend workout timelines to 3–5 years vs. 3–6 months in Ch.7 states.

YoY Growth: Nationwide Breadth¶

Year-over-Year Filing Change by State · Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025

In [11]:
show('06_state_yoy.png')

49 of 51 jurisdictions posted YoY increases. Breadth of this magnitude signals systemic financial pressure, not regional shocks.

Business Ch.11 Venue Concentration¶

Top Federal Districts · Forum-Shopping Map

In [12]:
show('05_ch11_districts.png')

The Key Ch.11 Venues¶

Jurisdiction Biz Ch.11 % of District Note
Texas Southern (Houston) 1,369 11.8% Dominant large-case venue
Texas Northern (Dallas) 996 7.4%
Delaware 412 19.0% Highest concentration nationally
New Jersey 610 4.2% Tri-state alternative
NY Eastern (Brooklyn) 524 4.7% Growing venue
NY Southern (Manhattan) 353 6.1% Traditional large-case venue

Delaware: 1 in 5 filings is a business reorganization vs. ~4% national average — reflecting its dominance as the preferred legal home for large public companies.

Risk Implications for Credit Professionals¶

1. Monitor household debt service, not unemployment TDSP and mortgage delinquency rates lead the cycle. Unemployment is a lagging distractor.

2. Business reorganization is structurally elevated Subchapter V permanently lowered the Ch.11 barrier. Pre-2020 baselines are obsolete.

3. Geographic exposure matters — especially in the South High-Ch.13 states mean 3–5 year workout timelines for mortgage and consumer portfolios.

Risk Implications (cont.)¶

4. The recovery is broad and re-accelerating 49/51 states growing. SQPY ticking back up to +13.6%. Normalization to pre-pandemic levels is not complete.

5. Exclude 2020–2022 from model calibration Stimulus effects created a once-in-a-generation false floor. Models trained on that period will systematically underpredict stress in the current environment.

Data & Methodology¶

Bankruptcy data — US Courts Quarterly Bankruptcy Filing Statistics

  • Tables F-2 (national/district, 12-month & 3-month), F-2.1 (monthly), F-5A (county-level)
  • 42 quarterly releases, 2016 Q3 – 2026 Q1 · 19,380 district-period rows · 4,941 county records

Macroeconomic data — FRED, Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

  • 12 series aggregated to quarter-end frequency · Coverage: 2000–present

Processing

  • Storage: DuckDB · All percentages use rolling 12-month denominators
  • SQPY growth used to remove bankruptcy filing seasonality

Live dashboard: bankruptcy-dashboard.vercel.app — updates automatically each quarter
